story.title: United by the Stage
story.body: Every year at the end of the school term, the students of Greenfield Academy would gather for the most important public event of the year — the Grand Performance Night. It was a complex event involving music, drama, poetry, and dance, and every member of the school was expected to perform at least one task on stage. For most students it was exciting. But for Nila, a quiet girl in the tenth grade, it was a source of enormous pressure. Nila had a real lack of confidence when it came to performing in public. The idea of standing on stage while a critic in the audience judged her every move made her want to block out the entire experience and hide somewhere far away. Her best friend Sana, who had the kind of warm and cheerful personality that could guide anyone through their darkest moments, noticed the change in Nila's behavior as the event drew near. Sana sat beside her during a break and read aloud from the script — a beautiful passage from a classic poem — to show her how the words could be felt rather than simply performed. 'Stop thinking about the critics,' Sana said. 'Think about the friendship we share and the public who came here to be entertained and moved — not to strike you down.' Those words struck something deep inside Nila. That evening, she began to exercise not just her voice but her courage. She read the passage over and over, letting each term and phrase become part of her. She asked her guide — an older student who had performed many times — to help her with the complex parts. She practiced until the probable became certain. On the night of the event, the hall was packed. The public filled every seat. When Nila's turn came, she walked onto the stage. For a moment the pressure was immense. But then she heard the sound of her classmates beginning to cheer softly from the sides, and she felt the support of every person who had guided her here. She performed the entire passage without a single block in her voice — steady, clear, and full of feeling. When she finished, the hall erupted. Even the most experienced critic in the room stood to applaud. After the show, Nila and Sana sat together under the stars. 'I never thought I could do it,' Nila said quietly. Sana smiled. 'That is what friendship is for — to unite us with the best version of ourselves, especially when we lack the courage to see it alone.'

listening.title: Two Friends Preparing for a Public Performance
listening.script:
A: The end of term event is in three days. Are you ready to perform?
B: Not really. I feel a lot of pressure every time I think about it.
A: That is probable. It is a complex thing to perform in public for the first time.
B: What if the critic in the front row strikes down everything I do?
A: A good critic looks for real effort, not perfection. Your task is to perform your best.
B: I keep reading the passage but some of the terms are still confusing me.
A: Let me guide you through it. We can work on the complex parts together.
B: Do you think I am fit enough to handle the pressure on stage?
A: You have been exercising your voice every day. That is the best preparation.
B: I just lack the confidence to walk out in front of the public.
A: That is where friendship helps. I will be in the front row cheering for you.
B: What if I block and forget the words?
A: Take a breath, pause, and the words will come back. The public is there to support you.
B: You really think they will cheer instead of judge?
A: Most of them will. And the ones who cheer will unite the whole hall behind you.
B: I suppose it is probable that I am stronger than I think.
A: Exactly. The lack of confidence is just a block in your mind. Remove it.
B: I will try. Will you help me run through the passage one more time?
A: Of course. That is what friendship is for. Let us go through every term together.
B: Thank you. I feel less pressure already.

speaking.topic: Talking About a Time You Performed or Did Something in Public
speaking.prompt: Describe a time you had to perform a task or do something in front of others. Use words like perform, pressure, public, support, cheer, lack, guide, friendship, fit, and term.
speaking.example: At the end of last term, our school held a public event where every student had to perform something on stage. I chose to read a passage from a poem I had written myself. At first, the pressure was immense. I had a real lack of confidence and was not sure I was fit enough mentally to stand in front of so many people. But my closest friend guided me through every complex part of the preparation. On the day, I heard my classmates begin to cheer when I stepped forward, and that support gave me the strength to perform without blocking. When I finished, even the critic on the panel gave me a positive term — he called my reading honest and heartfelt. It was one of the best experiences of my life, and it taught me that friendship and public support can unite a person with courage they did not know they had.
tips: Start by describing the event and what task you had to perform
Use pressure and lack to explain your feelings before the performance
Mention who guided or supported you and how friendship helped
Describe how the public reacted and whether they cheered
Use fit and exercise to talk about how you prepared
End with what you learned and how the term or experience changed you

conversation.scenario: Two classmates discussing an upcoming public event and how to support each other
